original_hypothesis.title: ERC20 unlock may silently fail or revert for non-standard tokens due to missing SafeERC20 handling
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The contract uses IERC20.transfer/transferFrom directly without SafeERC20. Tokens that return no boolean (or return false instead of reverting) can cause reverts (ABI decode) or silent failure, resulting in incorrect accounting: lockedToken.quantity is decreased but user may not receive tokens (if token returns false but doesn’t revert).
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Project documentation explicitly expects support for weird ERC20s. Direct calls are brittle and can break core user withdrawals, leading to trapped funds or accounting desync. This is practical and frequently exploited/triggered by real tokens.
deployment_assumptions: At least one configured token is non-standard (no return value, returns false, or has non-compliant behavior)
Admin intends to support such tokens per docs
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided that only strictly OZ-compliant ERC20s will be configured; docs explicitly state weird ERC20 support is expected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC20(_tokenContract).transferFrom(...) and IERC20(_tokenContract).transfer(...) used without SafeERC20

preconditions: A non-standard ERC20 is configured and active for locking
Users lock and later unlock that token
Token behavior includes missing return value / returning false / atypical revert behavior

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No SafeERC20 wrapper; return values not handled
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Unlock could decrement internal balance then fail to transfer (if token returns false without revert) or revert due to ABI decoding (no return)
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No balance-delta accounting; no require(success) pattern for low-level call

analysis_questions: Do any configured tokens in production return false (instead of revert) on transfer failures?
Is the IERC20 interface imported from OZ (expects bool), causing decode revert on no-return tokens?
Could silent failures lead to lockedToken.quantity decreasing without user receiving tokens?
validation_requirements: Test with a mock ERC20 that returns false on transfer
Test with a mock ERC20 that returns no value (USDT-style) and confirm behavior under solidity 0.8.25
Check whether any upstream contracts rely on lock/unlock success assumptions

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a token returns false but does not revert, attacker triggers unlock() to reduce their recorded locked quantity while receiving nothing, potentially causing later system inconsistencies (e.g., rewards/points accounting) and trapping funds. More commonly, users get stuck because transfers revert for non-standard tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Funds frozen for that token; possible accounting desync if transfers fail silently
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium (could be mitigated by migrating balances if supported)

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No evidence of weird-ERC20 compatibility tests despite documented requirement

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Compare internal accounting vs ERC20 balance movements in traces

validation_result.impact_summary: Non-standard ERC20 tokens (no-return-value like USDT) will cause lock/unlock to revert due to ABI decode failure; tokens returning false will cause silent transfer failure and accounting desync in unlock().
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The LockManager contract uses raw IERC20.transfer() and IERC20.transferFrom() calls without OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 wrapper. The IERC20 interface declares these functions as returning bool. Under Solidity 0.8.25, when the ABI decoder expects a bool return but receives 0 bytes (as with USDT-style tokens), the call reverts with a decoding error. This means any non-standard token that doesn't return a boolean value will be completely unusable for both lock and unlock operations, causing a denial of service. Additionally, tokens that return false instead of reverting on failure will cause the unlock function to silently fail the transfer while still decrementing lockedToken.quantity, creating an accounting desync where internal state shows reduced locked balance but no tokens were actually sent to the user.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code at lines ~290-295 in _lock() uses token.transferFrom(_tokenOwner, address(this), _quantity) without checking the return value and without SafeERC20. The code at lines ~329-333 in unlock() uses token.transfer(msg.sender, _quantity) without checking the return value and without SafeERC20. The import at line 4 is '@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/ERC20.sol' which contains the IERC20 interface expecting bool returns. SafeERC20 is not imported anywhere in the contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: transferFrom is called without SafeERC20.safeTransferFrom. For no-return-value tokens (USDT-style), this will revert due to ABI decode failure. For tokens returning false, the return value is silently ignored.

State is updated (quantity decremented) BEFORE the transfer call. If token.transfer returns false without reverting, the user's locked quantity is reduced but no tokens are sent. The return value of transfer() is not checked and SafeERC20 is not used.

calling_sequence: 4a. If token returns no data (USDT-style): ABI decoder fails, entire tx reverts, state change rolled back - DoS
4b. If token returns false: call succeeds from Solidity perspective, return value ignored, function completes with accounting desync
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: In unlock(), the lockedToken.quantity is decremented at line ~325 before the ERC20 transfer at line ~333. The IERC20 interface from OpenZeppelin declares transfer() as returning bool. Solidity 0.8.25 ABI decoder requires the return data to decode correctly: 0-byte return causes revert (DoS for no-return tokens), while a false return is silently ignored (accounting desync for false-returning tokens). The same pattern exists in _lock() for transferFrom, though the impact there is that the contract records locked tokens it never received (if transferFrom returns false without reverting).

deployment_assumptions_checked: At least one configured token is non-standard (no return value, returns false, or has non-compliant behavior)
Admin intends to support such tokens per docs
config_validation.deployment_evidence: configure-token-test.ts shows only USDB, WETH, and ETH (address(0)) being configured. These are standard tokens on Blast chain that return bool on transfer/transferFrom. However, configureToken() allows admin to add any arbitrary token address at any time, and no deployment scripts restrict this. No evidence of docs requiring weird ERC20 support was found in the scripts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Scenario 1 (DoS): Admin configures a USDT-style token (no return value). Any user who locks that token can never unlock it because token.transfer() will revert during ABI decoding. Funds are permanently trapped. Scenario 2 (Accounting desync): A token that returns false on insufficient balance (instead of reverting) is configured. If the contract's actual balance is lower than expected (due to other issues or external manipulation), unlock() would decrement lockedToken.quantity without sending tokens. The user loses recorded locked balance. Scenario 1 is highly realistic if non-standard tokens are ever configured; Scenario 2 is less common but the code defect exists.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH - If a non-standard ERC20 token is configured by admin: (1) DoS: All users' funds locked in that token become permanently trapped, as both lock and unlock operations revert. (2) Accounting desync: For false-returning tokens, users may have their locked quantity reduced without receiving tokens. The defect exists in the code regardless of current token configuration and represents a latent risk that materializes upon admin action.
recommended_mitigations: Import and use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library: import '@openzeppelin/contracts/token/ERC20/utils/SafeERC20.sol'; using SafeERC20 for IERC20;
Replace token.transfer() with token.safeTransfer() in unlock()
Consider adding a token whitelist validation or interface check in configureToken() to ensure only compliant tokens are added
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a mock ERC20 that returns no value on transfer/transferFrom (USDT-style). 2. Admin configures it via configureToken(). 3. User approves and calls lock() - expect revert. 4. Deploy another mock that returns false on transfer failure. 5. Fund contract with fewer tokens than expected. 6. Call unlock() and verify lockedToken.quantity decreases while user balance unchanged.
uncertainties: The exact tokens that will be configured in production are not fully specified in deployment scripts
Whether the project documentation truly requires weird ERC20 support could not be verified from available scripts
On Blast chain specifically, USDB and WETH are standard - the risk depends on future admin token configuration decisions
additional_testing_needed: Test with a no-return-value ERC20 mock (USDT-style) to confirm ABI decode revert
Test with a false-returning ERC20 mock to confirm silent failure in unlock
Verify actual token implementations on Blast chain (USDB, WETH) return bool correctly
Check if any planned future token additions include non-standard tokens
